project.description: The A11Y Project is a community-driven effort to make digital accessibility easier.
project.name: a11yproject.com

target.title: feat: add WCAG levels
target.description: Closes #1209 ## Summary This PR adds the WCAG level to each checklist item so it's easier to know what checks to prioritize when trying to make a site accessible. ## Notes / Questions * Do you think that the placement of the new information is correct / the best way to display it? I tried putting it in the part of the checklist item that is always visible but there there was a concern with responsiveness and the full `Level: AAA` was taking too much space. * @ericwbailey you [mentioned](https://github.com/a11yproject/a11yproject.com/issues/1209#issuecomment-799887433) in the issue that some checklist items might represent multiple success criterions. At least from what I saw each checklist item has only one WCAG link and each of those links only had one WCAG level asociated. That being said, I know for a fact that for example there are different contrast requirements for different levels but those don't seems to be covered in the checklist, just mention the one for level AA that is the minimum. Any thoughts on this? do you think that it's ok as I put it? * There was one checklist item that linked to a WCAG technique instead of a "Understanding WCAG" rule and didn't have a level asociated to for that one I put `Level: doesn't apply`. I thought about putting nothing at all but then it would have looked like it was missing. ## Preview

project.description: 💄 Accessible light and dark syntax highlighting themes
project.name: a11y-syntax-highlighting

target.title: Tm Theme Editor
target.description:
Hi!

Good day to you! I greatly appreciate the efforts and work going into the a11y-syntax-highlighting project. I have seen much positive feedback on A11y Dark theme on Carbon and I share that sentiment. A11y Dark is one of the best themes I have seen in a while. Are you aware of any efforts to add A11y Dark theme to the Tm Theme Editor Website? 

https://tmtheme-editor.herokuapp.com/#!/editor/theme/Monokai

I would be happy to assist to build one but would need to know all of the possible scope names and associated foreground/background hex values. I would not want to make any assumptions and diverge from the appropriate color theme.

Thanks so much!

project.description: 🌐 A webring for digital accessibility practitioners.
project.name: a11y-webring.club

target.title: Add verification functions
target.description:
This PR adds two different implementations of a way to verify members of the webring. 

The first, `verify.mjs` simply makes sure that the listed URL only returns a response in the `2xx` range. This is a quick spot check to make sure that the site owner doesn't redirect to a different location. The second, `verifyDNS.mjs` is slightly more complicated. It verifies, using DNS TXT records, whether a site owner controls their own domain by requiring a TXT record on the listed hostname. Both of these functions use Netlify's scheduled functions feature. `verify` runs daily and `verifyDNS` runs weekly. Obviously that timing can be changed by updating the value in `netlify.toml`.

Neither of these implementations solves the question as to what to do if one of these functions actually encounters that scenario. I don't know much about Netlify or whether it has logging or alerting functionality. Hopefully this helps in the discussion in #33.

step_1.title: [Post]: What is Semantic HTML?
step_1.description:
## Your idea

I'd like to write a post on what semantic HTML is, and why it's beneficial. I'll go into what you get when you use semantic HTML.

## Outline (optional)

1. What is Semantic HTML
    a. Structure
    b. Meaning
2. What you Get with Semantic HTML
3. References and Further Reading

## Additional information (optional)

I used several posts as references for writing this. One that helped guide the way was Léonie Watson's [Understanding semantics](https://tink.uk/understanding-semantics/). It's possible that my post is too similar. It's worth digging deep to make sure it adds something substantial to the existing content already out there.

step_1.title: [Post] Don't Wait Until Launch: Why Accessibility Testing Early On Can Save You Time and Money
step_1.description:
Thank you for your interest in writing a post! Please fill out the following information:

## Your idea

> Please describe the post you'd like to write in 2–4 sentences, and how that post would be a good match for The A11Y Project.

I'd like to write a post about the importance of accessibility testing early on in the development process. I will discuss why it's important, how it can save time and money down the line, and provide best practices for incorporating accessibility testing into an organization's workflow.

## Outline (optional)
> Optionally include a proposed outline for the post.

I. Introduction 
    a. Why is accessibility important? 
    b. Overview of the benefits of accessibility testing early on in development 
II. Benefits of Accessibility Testing Early On in Development 
    a. Improved user experience with fewer errors 
    b. Saves time and money by catching issues earlier on 
    c. Helps achieve compliance 
III. Best Practices for Accessibility Testing Early On in Development 
    a. Automation-driven accessibility testing 
    b. Manual testing by experienced accessibility testers 
    c. Accessibility checks during code review process 
IV. Conclusion 
    a. Summary and takeaway points 
    b. Resources for further learning 
    c. Contact info for accessibility help and advice 

## Additional information (optional)

> Is there anything else we should know?

I have extensive experience in the digital accessibility field and have been a part of several successful projects that implemented accessibility testing early on. I am confident that this post would be both informative and useful to readers. Thank you for considering me as an author!

## Terms
- [ Yes] I have read, and agree to follow this project's [Contributing Guidelines](https://www.a11yproject.com/contributing-guidelines/)
- [ Yes] I have read, and agree to follow this project's [Content Style Guide](https://www.a11yproject.com/content-style-guide/)
- [ Yes] I have read, and agree to follow this project's [Code of Conduct](https://www.a11yproject.com/code-of-conduct/)

step_1.title: `npm start` does not run `gulp-watch` (Windows)
step_1.description:
**Description**
I can sorta build the project, but I'm stuck on two issues:

1. Doing `npm start` does not actually host it locally for me, but it does seem to generate all the website files.
2. When I open the index html file, it looks like it's missing all the styles for some reason.

**Environment**
I've met the criteria mentioned under 'Requirements':
- Windows 10 Home 64 bit
- Terminal
- git version 2.39.1.windows.1
- Node 16.0.0 installed through nvm
- VS Code 1.75.1

**Steps to reproduce**
Download and clone project, main branch `npm install` then `npm start`.

**Expected Results**
I expected to see the site build and run on localhost:(something).

**Actual Results**

1. I don't see a line in my terminal that says `Local:`
2. If I open the index.html file manually, the site looks weird. It doesn't have any styling on it.

![image](/uploads/68478f4942830125c077c8817bf8c783/221386612-7bcf9feb-e4bc-45ae-8c18-c3fc40c1f3e2.png)

step_1.title: Link to WCAG 2.1 instead of 2.0?
step_1.description:
### Discussed in https://github.com/a11yproject/a11yproject.com/discussions/1459
Originally posted by **robinmetral** August 10, 2022 Can we link to the WCAG 2.1 website instead of WCAG 2.0? I'm not sure if this is consistent throughout the site, but I'm seeing it on e.g. the checklist links to success criteria. For instance, users are sent to [3.1.5 Reading Level (WCAG 2.0)](https://www.w3.org/TR/UNDERSTANDING-WCAG20/meaning-supplements.html) instead of [3.1.5 Reading Level (WCAG 2.1)](https://www.w3.org/WAI/WCAG21/Understanding/reading-level.html). Am I missing a reason why these should continue to link to 2.0?

step_1.title: [Post] What is WAI? The Web Accessibility Initiative
step_1.description:
Thank you for your interest in writing a post! Please fill out the following information:

## Your idea

I will be writing a post on WAI, Web Accessibility Initiative. I will focus on its history and importance.

## Outline (optional)

- What is the World Wide Consortium?
- What is the Web Accessibility Initiative?
- WCAG and WAI-Aria Recommendations:
- Working groups/interest groups

step_1.title: [Post] Bake accessibility into your design and development process
step_1.description:
## Your idea
Taking more of an operational perspective on accessibility, this post would explore some of the practical ways in which moments of accessibility can be created in the software design and development process and how the team can make accessibility part of their day-to-day work in a sustainable way.

## Outline (optional)
- Encourage consistency with checklists and playbooks
- Design for diversity with persona spectrums and inclusive lenses
- Get a second opinion with design crits and code reviews
- Formalize expectations in the Definition of Ready and Definition of Done
- Combine the benefits of automated checks and human testing

## Additional information (optional)

step_1.title: [Post] New Post: Text color contrasts on dynamic backgrounds
step_1.description:
Thank you for your interest in writing a post! Please fill out the following information:

## Text Contrast on Dynamic Backgrounds

This post could center around design and styling techniques to ensure color contrast for foreground text with dynamic background colors, images, videos, overflowing elements, etc. Providing a simple set of know tools / techniques, tricks of the trade, and best practice guidelines, this post can provide designers and developers with simple solutions for text overlaying complex and / or changing backgrounds.

## Outline (optional)

* Brief Overview (mostly definitions and links to other color contrast docs)
* Obstacles of accessibly overlaying text on visually dynamic
* The Solutions (best / most general cases)
* Implementations (design tools, HTML / CSS, plugins / libraries)
* Further Readings

## Additional information (optional)

Hello, as a design technologist I have dealt with this issue many times in my career and would love to help discuss / document / establish some definitive best practices!

## Terms
- [x] I have read, and agree to follow this project's [Contributing Guidelines](https://www.a11yproject.com/contributing-guidelines/)
- [x] I have read, and agree to follow this project's [Content Style Guide](https://www.a11yproject.com/content-style-guide/)
- [x] I have read, and agree to follow this project's [Code of Conduct](https://www.a11yproject.com/code-of-conduct/)

step_1.title: Investigate if <details>/<summary> is a good choice for our Checklist
step_1.description:
We currently use the `details` and `summary` elements for [our Checklist page](https://www.a11yproject.com/checklist/). These element allow for a native expand/collapse experience, without the use of JavaScript.

Our checklist items do not use heading elements, which was a concern voiced in this [thread on the web-a11y Slack](https://web-a11y.slack.com/archives/C071U5Y3H/p1643052064103100). There is also mention of concerns around interactive content placed within the `summary` element, which we do use for both WCAG links and permalinks.

Additionally, [this repo by Melanie Sumner](https://github.com/MelSumner/test-details) highlights other potential issues.

We should revisit our usage of `details` and `summary` elements to see if they are usable, or if an ARIA construct would be a more desirable replacement.

step_1.title: In error state, focused controls take on purple or red-and-purple focus indicator
step_1.description: ## Summary When the contact form is in an error state, we intend to show a solid red focus indicator on inputs. In Chromium and Firefox browser, we render our custom purple focus indicator mixed with red. On Safari, we render a solid purple indicator. Both behaviors are undesirable, and the distinction between them probably comes from Chromium/Firefox's switch to `:focus-visible` as the default focus heuristic. Focused text control in Safari: Focused text control in Chromium Edge (MacOS)

step_1.title: The process for writing for us is both scattered and buried
step_1.description:
Compensation information is only listed on [our Contribute page](https://www.a11yproject.com/contribute/#posts). Post requirements are hidden away deep in [our Contributing Guidelines](https://www.a11yproject.com/contributing-guidelines/#posts).

We should consolidate this information to its own page, potentially referencing an approach similar to [Smashing Magazine](https://www.smashingmagazine.com/write-for-us/) and other similar publications.

step_1.title: Our non-GitHub editorial process is opaque
step_1.description:
We're still in the process of figuring this out, but that doesn't mean the existing process should be documented.

Big picture, we'll highlight:

1. What happens if your pitch is rejected
    1. What you can do about it
2. What happens if your pitch is accepted
    1. What the editing process looks like
    2. What the publishing process looks like
    3. What happens after publishing

step_1.title: Article: Starting a design with accessibility
step_1.description:
Title up for debate! "Designing with accessibility"?

## Description

A prequel to [Reviewing a design for accessibility](https://www.a11yproject.com/posts/2021-08-14-reviewing-a-design-for-accessibility/), shifting further left. Three approaches we can take to design more accessible things:

- do a text-only design;
- do an HTML-only design;
- go off The Happy Path.

Each of the three will have a "What to do" bulleted list and a "Why to do it" highlighting the accessibility benefits of the approach.

## Would you like to write the article or request that it be written?

I would like to write it please!

I have a [very rough first draft in this gist](https://gist.github.com/SteveBarnett/f92d8e9c6d9f9d134763ebf505aeaf30).

step_1.title: We should provide syntactic sugar for creating figures in Markdown
step_1.description:
## Problem Currently, post authors have to write out full HTML to generate a figure. Take this example from one of our posts (image alt text truncated for brevity): ```html ``` Post authors must understand enough HTML to be able to duplicate this structure when making their own figures, and be comfortable with changes to suit their specific case. They have to know - to keep the figure's `role` - to replace the `img` tag's attributes - to repeat their `figcaption`'s text content in the figure `aria-label`, but strip out any special characters ## Solution Use a `markdown-it` plugin so that authors can write markdown that produces a `figure` to our specifications. ### Option 1: Implicit figures We could overload the default markdown shorthand for an image, so that it produces a figure if (and only if) the image is on its own line in the markdown. This overload would convert the image's `title` attribute to the figure's `figcaption` element This markown: ```md ![Alt text](path/to/img.png "Caption") This image is inline with some text, and that's a little weird ![Alt text](path/to/img.png "Caption"). ``` Would render this html: ```html

This image is inline with some text, and that's a little weird .

``` Note that the markdown that was converted into a `figure` did not keep the image `title`. The markdown that remained an `img` did keep its title. ➕ Known syntax; no need to learn new things ➖ Because this overloads known syntax, experienced markdown writers could be confused ### Option 2: Figure blocks We could create a wholly new syntax that renders markdown into a `figure` tag. This markdown: ```md +++ Caption + ![Alt text](path/to/img.png) +++ ``` Would render this HTML: ```html ``` ➕ No overloading existing syntax ➖ Slightly more verbose

step_1.title: We should provide a callout card component for emphasizing content
step_1.description: ## What is it? Callout cards use visual affordances (like borders, lead words, or other shapes) to indicate that a pierce of content is important. The effect usually results in the content appearing as if it is a card laid on top of the site. Importantly, there are no HTML elements or ARIA attributes to indicate that content needs emphasis. Callout cards must rely on content and visual affordance – ideally, both. ## What we have now The closest thing The A11Y Project has to a callout card is our visual design for a blockquote. This does not work as a callout card, because its visual affordances (and underlying semantics) invoke the concept of a quote. Content callouts are not quotes. ## Some callout card concepts in the wild CSS tricks draws a 3-sided orange border around its callout cards, and introduces their content with "Hey!". Shopify's Polaris framework uses a four-sided card, as well as images, to draw reader attention.

step_1.title: [Post] Don't Wait Until Launch: Why Accessibility Testing Early On Can Save You Time and Money
step_1.description:
Thank you for your interest in writing a post! Please fill out the following information:

## Your idea

> Please describe the post you'd like to write in 2–4 sentences, and how that post would be a good match for The A11Y Project.

I'd like to write a post about the importance of accessibility testing early on in the development process. I will discuss why it's important, how it can save time and money down the line, and provide best practices for incorporating accessibility testing into an organization's workflow.

## Outline (optional)
> Optionally include a proposed outline for the post.

I. Introduction 
    a. Why is accessibility important? 
    b. Overview of the benefits of accessibility testing early on in development 
II. Benefits of Accessibility Testing Early On in Development 
    a. Improved user experience with fewer errors 
    b. Saves time and money by catching issues earlier on 
    c. Helps achieve compliance 
III. Best Practices for Accessibility Testing Early On in Development 
    a. Automation-driven accessibility testing 
    b. Manual testing by experienced accessibility testers 
    c. Accessibility checks during code review process 
IV. Conclusion 
    a. Summary and takeaway points 
    b. Resources for further learning 
    c. Contact info for accessibility help and advice 

## Additional information (optional)

> Is there anything else we should know?

I have extensive experience in the digital accessibility field and have been a part of several successful projects that implemented accessibility testing early on. I am confident that this post would be both informative and useful to readers. Thank you for considering me as an author!

## Terms
- [ Yes] I have read, and agree to follow this project's [Contributing Guidelines](https://www.a11yproject.com/contributing-guidelines/)

step_1.title: `npm start` does not run `gulp-watch` (Windows)
step_1.description:
**Description**
I can sorta build the project, but I'm stuck on two issues:

1. Doing `npm start` does not actually host it locally for me, but it does seem to generate all the website files.
2. When I open the index html file, it looks like it's missing all the styles for some reason.

**Environment**
I've met the criteria mentioned under 'Requirements':
- Windows 10 Home 64 bit
- Terminal
- git version 2.39.1.windows.1
- Node 16.0.0 installed through nvm
- VS Code 1.75.1

**Steps to reproduce**
Download and clone project, main branch `npm install` then `npm start`.

**Expected Results**
I expected to see the site build and run on localhost:(something).

**Actual Results**

1. I don't see a line in my terminal that says `Local:`
2. If I open the index.html file manually, the site looks weird. It doesn't have any styling on it.

![image](/uploads/68478f4942830125c077c8817bf8c783/221386612-7bcf9feb-e4bc-45ae-8c18-c3fc40c1f3e2.png)

step_1.title: Link to WCAG 2.1 instead of 2.0?
step_1.description:
### Discussed in https://github.com/a11yproject/a11yproject.com/discussions/1459
Originally posted by **robinmetral** August 10, 2022 Can we link to the WCAG 2.1 website instead of WCAG 2.0? I'm not sure if this is consistent throughout the site, but I'm seeing it on e.g. the checklist links to success criteria. For instance, users are sent to [3.1.5 Reading Level (WCAG 2.0)](https://www.w3.org/TR/UNDERSTANDING-WCAG20/meaning-supplements.html) instead of [3.1.5 Reading Level (WCAG 2.1)](https://www.w3.org/WAI/WCAG21/Understanding/reading-level.html). Am I missing a reason why these should continue to link to 2.0?

step_1.title: [Post] New Post: Text color contrasts on dynamic backgrounds
step_1.description:
Thank you for your interest in writing a post! Please fill out the following information:

## Text Contrast on Dynamic Backgrounds

This post could center around design and styling techniques to ensure color contrast for foreground text with dynamic background colors, images, videos, overflowing elements, etc. Providing a simple set of know tools / techniques, tricks of the trade, and best practice guidelines, this post can provide designers and developers with simple solutions for text overlaying complex and / or changing backgrounds.

## Outline (optional)

* Brief Overview (mostly definitions and links to other color contrast docs)
* Obstacles of accessibly overlaying text on visually dynamic
* The Solutions (best / most general cases)
* Implementations (design tools, HTML / CSS, plugins / libraries)
* Further Readings

## Additional information (optional)

Hello, as a design technologist I have dealt with this issue many times in my career and would love to help discuss / document / establish some definitive best practices!

## Terms
- [x] I have read, and agree to follow this project's [Contributing Guidelines](https://www.a11yproject.com/contributing-guidelines/)
- [x] I have read, and agree to follow this project's [Content Style Guide](https://www.a11yproject.com/content-style-guide/)
- [x] I have read, and agree to follow this project's [Code of Conduct](https://www.a11yproject.com/code-of-conduct/)

step_1.title: Investigate if <details>/<summary> is a good choice for our Checklist
step_1.description:
We currently use the `details` and `summary` elements for [our Checklist page](https://www.a11yproject.com/checklist/). These element allow for a native expand/collapse experience, without the use of JavaScript.

Our checklist items do not use heading elements, which was a concern voiced in this [thread on the web-a11y Slack](https://web-a11y.slack.com/archives/C071U5Y3H/p1643052064103100). There is also mention of concerns around interactive content placed within the `summary` element, which we do use for both WCAG links and permalinks.

Additionally, [this repo by Melanie Sumner](https://github.com/MelSumner/test-details) highlights other potential issues.

We should revisit our usage of `details` and `summary` elements to see if they are usable, or if an ARIA construct would be a more desirable replacement.

step_1.title: In error state, focused controls take on purple or red-and-purple focus indicator
step_1.description: ## Summary When the contact form is in an error state, we intend to show a solid red focus indicator on inputs. In Chromium and Firefox browser, we render our custom purple focus indicator mixed with red. On Safari, we render a solid purple indicator. Both behaviors are undesirable, and the distinction between them probably comes from Chromium/Firefox's switch to `:focus-visible` as the default focus heuristic. Focused text control in Safari: Focused text control in Chromium Edge (MacOS)

step_1.title: The process for writing for us is both scattered and buried
step_1.description:
Compensation information is only listed on [our Contribute page](https://www.a11yproject.com/contribute/#posts). Post requirements are hidden away deep in [our Contributing Guidelines](https://www.a11yproject.com/contributing-guidelines/#posts).

We should consolidate this information to its own page, potentially referencing an approach similar to [Smashing Magazine](https://www.smashingmagazine.com/write-for-us/) and other similar publications.

step_1.title: Our non-GitHub editorial process is opaque
step_1.description:
We're still in the process of figuring this out, but that doesn't mean the existing process should be documented.

Big picture, we'll highlight:

1. What happens if your pitch is rejected
    1. What you can do about it
2. What happens if your pitch is accepted
    1. What the editing process looks like
    2. What the publishing process looks like
    3. What happens after publishing

step_1.title: Article: Starting a design with accessibility
step_1.description:
Title up for debate! "Designing with accessibility"?

## Description

A prequel to [Reviewing a design for accessibility](https://www.a11yproject.com/posts/2021-08-14-reviewing-a-design-for-accessibility/), shifting further left. Three approaches we can take to design more accessible things:

- do a text-only design;
- do an HTML-only design;
- go off The Happy Path.

Each of the three will have a "What to do" bulleted list and a "Why to do it" highlighting the accessibility benefits of the approach.

## Would you like to write the article or request that it be written?

I would like to write it please!

I have a [very rough first draft in this gist](https://gist.github.com/SteveBarnett/f92d8e9c6d9f9d134763ebf505aeaf30).

step_1.title: We should provide syntactic sugar for creating figures in Markdown
step_1.description:
## Problem Currently, post authors have to write out full HTML to generate a figure. Take this example from one of our posts (image alt text truncated for brevity): ```html ``` Post authors must understand enough HTML to be able to duplicate this structure when making their own figures, and be comfortable with changes to suit their specific case. They have to know - to keep the figure's `role` - to replace the `img` tag's attributes - to repeat their `figcaption`'s text content in the figure `aria-label`, but strip out any special characters ## Solution Use a `markdown-it` plugin so that authors can write markdown that produces a `figure` to our specifications. ### Option 1: Implicit figures We could overload the default markdown shorthand for an image, so that it produces a figure if (and only if) the image is on its own line in the markdown. This overload would convert the image's `title` attribute to the figure's `figcaption` element This markown: ```md ![Alt text](path/to/img.png "Caption") This image is inline with some text, and that's a little weird ![Alt text](path/to/img.png "Caption"). ``` Would render this html: ```html

This image is inline with some text, and that's a little weird .

``` Note that the markdown that was converted into a `figure` did not keep the image `title`. The markdown that remained an `img` did keep its title. ➕ Known syntax; no need to learn new things ➖ Because this overloads known syntax, experienced markdown writers could be confused ### Option 2: Figure blocks We could create a wholly new syntax that renders markdown into a `figure` tag. This markdown: ```md +++ Caption + ![Alt text](path/to/img.png) +++ ``` Would render this HTML: ```html ``` ➕ No overloading existing syntax ➖ Slightly more verbose

step_1.title: We should provide a callout card component for emphasizing content
step_1.description: ## What is it? Callout cards use visual affordances (like borders, lead words, or other shapes) to indicate that a pierce of content is important. The effect usually results in the content appearing as if it is a card laid on top of the site. Importantly, there are no HTML elements or ARIA attributes to indicate that content needs emphasis. Callout cards must rely on content and visual affordance – ideally, both. ## What we have now The closest thing The A11Y Project has to a callout card is our visual design for a blockquote. This does not work as a callout card, because its visual affordances (and underlying semantics) invoke the concept of a quote. Content callouts are not quotes. ## Some callout card concepts in the wild CSS tricks draws a 3-sided orange border around its callout cards, and introduces their content with "Hey!". Shopify's Polaris framework uses a four-sided card, as well as images, to draw reader attention.

tasks.answer:
## Most Recent Open Issues for a11yproject/a11yproject.com

Here are the 20 most recent open issues, ordered from newest to oldest:

### Post Proposals
1. **#1533** – *[Post] HOWTO: Ajax with ARIA-LIVE* — by Paul McFedries (Mar 22, 2023) – Covers ARIA-LIVE for dynamic Ajax content. Assigned to Roshan Jossy. `claimed`
2. **#1530** – *[Post] What is Semantic HTML?* — by David A. Kennedy (Mar 19, 2023) – Explains semantic HTML and its benefits. `claimed`
3. **#1529** – *[Post] Don't Wait Until Launch: Why Accessibility Testing Early On Can Save You Time and Money* — by John Nyaruiru (Mar 13, 2023) – Importance of early accessibility testing. `claimed`
4. **#1526** – *[Post] HOWTO: Use JAWS on Windows* — by ilknureren (Mar 7, 2023) – Guide to using the JAWS screen reader. `claimed`
5. **#1509** – *[Post] Getting Started with TalkBack* — by Cooper Hollmaier (Feb 12, 2023) – Intro to Android's TalkBack screen reader. `claimed`
6. **#1491** – *[Post] Accessibility in Content Creation and UX Writing* — by Victoria Ottah (Nov 30, 2022) – Accessibility for content creators/UX writers. **Terms not agreed to.**
7. **#1449** – *[Post] What is WAI?* — by ilknureren (Jul 18, 2022) – History and importance of the Web Accessibility Initiative. `claimed`, assigned to ilknureren.
8. **#1447** – *[Post] Bake Accessibility into Your Design and Development Process* — by Matt Obee (Jul 4, 2022) – Operational approaches to embedding accessibility. `claimed`, assigned to Roshan Jossy.
9. **#1443** – *[Post] Text Color Contrasts on Dynamic Backgrounds* — by Conor (May 28, 2022) – Techniques for accessible text over dynamic backgrounds. `claimed`
10. **#1334** – *Article: Starting a Design with Accessibility* — by Steve Barnett (Aug 21, 2021) – A prequel to "Reviewing a Design for Accessibility." `claimed`

### Bugs
11. **#1519** – *`npm start` does not run `gulp-watch` (Windows)* — by Philip Wong (Feb 26, 2023) – Build/dev server not working on Windows 10; styles missing.
12. **#1478** – *[Bug] 404s, bad hosts, timeouts for linked URLs* — by Roshan Jossy (Oct 3, 2022) – **33 broken links** found across the site. Only 1 of 34 checklist items fixed so far. `good first issue`, `help wanted`
13. **#1419** – *Investigate if `<details>`/`<summary>` is a good choice for the Checklist* — by Byte Blaze (Mar 2, 2022) – Concerns about accessibility of the expand/collapse pattern used on the checklist page. `help wanted`
14. **#1408** – *Focus indicator bug in error state* — by EJ Mason (Jan 31, 2022) – Focused form controls show incorrect purple/red indicator across browsers. `claimed`, assigned to EJ Mason.

### Features & Enhancements
15. **#1521** – *[Feature] Add a broken link checker to CI/CD* — by Philip Wong (Feb 28, 2023) – Automate link checking in the pipeline. `help wanted`
16. **#1302** – *Provide syntactic sugar for creating figures in Markdown* — by EJ Mason (Jun 29, 2021) – Simplify how authors create `<figure>` elements in posts. `claimed`
17. **#1295** – *Provide a callout card component for emphasizing content* — by EJ Mason (Jun 17, 2021) – Design a visual callout card distinct from blockquotes. `claimed`

### Content & Process
18. **#1460** – *Link to WCAG 2.1 instead of 2.0?* — by Byte Blaze (Aug 11, 2022) – Checklist still links to WCAG 2.0 success criteria. `claimed`
19. **#1360** – *The process for writing for us is scattered and buried* — by Byte Blaze (Oct 11, 2021) – Consolidate contributor/writing info into a dedicated page.
20. **#1359** – *Our non-GitHub editorial process is opaque* — by Byte Blaze (Oct 11, 2021) – Document the pitch → editing → publishing workflow.

### Summary
- **Most issues are post proposals** for new accessibility articles, many labeled `claimed`.
- **Key bugs** include broken links across the site (#1478, with 33 URLs to fix), a Windows build issue, and UI focus indicator problems.
- **Help wanted** items include the broken link checker feature (#1521), fixing broken links (#1478), and investigating the `<details>`/`<summary>` pattern (#1419).
